text: I experience emotions at a high volume — when something hits me, it hits hard.
My emotional responses tend to linger long after the triggering event has passed.
I often feel flooded by the intensity of my own reactions.
When something excites me, the rush of energy is almost overwhelming.
I find that my body reacts strongly to emotional situations — racing heart, flushed skin, tight chest.
I can still feel the emotional residue of an argument hours or even days later.
I experience joy with an intensity that sometimes surprises the people around me.
A beautiful piece of music can bring me to tears almost instantly.
I feel things so deeply that it can be physically exhausting.
When I'm upset, the feeling saturates my entire body.
I go from calm to fully activated in a matter of seconds.
My emotional peaks are dramatically higher than most people's seem to be.
I have a vivid inner emotional landscape that is constantly shifting.
Small moments — a sunset, a kind word — can produce a surprisingly intense reaction in me.
I find it difficult to dial down my emotional intensity once it has ramped up.
I experience disappointment as a full-body event, not just a passing thought.
My passions tend to consume me rather than simmer in the background.
I notice that my emotional reactions are often more intense than what the situation calls for.
When I'm excited about something, I feel it in every cell of my body.
Even minor stressors can set off a cascade of intense internal sensations.
I hold the view that feeling things intensely is simply how I'm wired.
I believe my nervous system runs at a higher baseline than most people's.
After a stressful event, it takes me a long time to return to my emotional baseline.
I find that a single upsetting comment can color my entire day.

I wake up some mornings already buzzing with emotional energy before anything has happened.
When I feel gratitude, it swells inside me until I can barely contain it.
I am prone to sudden surges of emotion that catch me off guard.
I feel that my emotional thermostat is set higher than average.

After receiving bad news, I can feel the shock reverberating through my body for hours.

I sometimes feel overwhelmed by the sheer intensity of being alive.
I find that transitions between emotional states happen abruptly for me, not gradually.
